sentences.text: Well, there was really no way of breaking out of the-- of the existence, unless we, we did something that we were really not supposed to do.
So Munich was famous for its-- for its Fasching, which is the carnival, the Mardi Gras.
And there were wonderful balls in the big beer halls where everybody danced and had a great time.
And we used to go there once a year, or twice during that time to take part in it.
And in 1934, I decided that I was going to go with a mask and risk it.
And I did.
And actually, not that-- I didn't have a good time, personally.

But I could watch the people walking up the stairs, down the stairs, the masses of people dancing and the music, and the whole atmosphere, which was really unique-- a total release of, of, of people's feelings for, for joy and freedom, and, and, and have fun.
And I, I was recognized.
In spite of my mask, I was recognized by a former schoolmate, classmate of mine, who was, was a German, Aryan classmate.
And he said to me, come with me.
I show you-- [LAUGHS] I show you the leader of the SS and his boyfriends.
And there was Rohm sitting there on a big table with his-- with his friends.
That was in January 1934.
And by the-- by June 1934, Rohm and a lot of these other SS leaders were shot.
They were killed by Hitler, under the pretense that they were homosexuals, which they actually were.
Because there were only boys that he was sitting there in a very-- these balls are very heterosexual.
Everybody dances, and people kissed and embraced each other.
And this was one of the sensational things that I remember, seeing that fat guy sitting there with all the young boys.
And the same man, a few months later, was dead.
Also, he was one of the founders of the most faithful, early adherence of Hitler.
Yeah, I did.
Also an encounter in 1933, in the very early days when the Nazis had just gotten into power and had just made laws that-- they were-- they were extremely occupied with sex.
I had-- you could feel that that was a very important thing, the, the fear of the fact that-- the fear or the envy-- I don't know what it was-- that Jews could contaminate the German race.
And it was a day in which I had a-- I took a date with me to a cafe near Nuremberg.
And that cafe was outside on a little mountain on a very steep hill.
And I was driving my father's car and was very concerned about the car.
I wouldn't want it-- better bring it home in good condition.
And after I made my way up this rocky road with the girl in the car, I saw when I got up there all these black Mercedes cars, perhaps five or six of them, that were trademarks of Nazi leadership.
And I thought to myself, I should not stay, but to turn around now-- and it was so steep.
I didn't know how to turn around, so I, I didn't want to say anything to the girl.
And we went ahead and we sat down, and we ordered a little supper.

And a few tables away from us, with his entourage, sat the infamous leader of all anti-Semites, the, the creator of which was the most hateful paper that had existed in Nuremberg, anti-Semitic paper-- Julius Streicher, who by then had become the Gauleiter, which meant the provincial leader of Nuremberg and the Nazis.
He was sitting there in his black SS uniform with brown and black shirts together on a long table.
Well, I said to the young lady that went-- was with me, don't look in this direction.
Let's just concentrate on our meal.
But not long-- it didn't take any time when one of these brown shirt soldiers came over to our table, walked over to us and said to me, you, are your a Jew?
I said, yes.
So he said, and the lady, is the lady Jewish?
At least, at that moment, I knew what they were aiming at.
And before I could say anything, the young girl who got up and said, yes.
She said, I am Jewish.
This is my-- such and such is my name, and I'm the daughter of such and such.
And I'm from Furth.
And he said, Heil Hitler, and turned around and went away.
Well, this was 1933.
We were not, at that time, not used to this treatment yet.
It was only a few months since they had been in power.
And the humiliation and the-- I mean, the people looked at us from the other tables.
So I-- the question was, are we leaving, or are we going to finish?
And I said, let's sit down and finish our meal.
We-- it's-- I felt at that moment that was better, so that's what we did.
And then we drove home.
I still remember it, a nice summer evening.
It was-- that's when I decided that I had to leave Germany.
It took that.
And, I will never forget that.
Yes, they did very much.
They did very much support it.
My father, in fact, contacted a distant cousin.
No, it was a first cousin, who lived at that time in Cincinnati, who had been coming-- he was-- had gone to America already many, many years before.
And through him, I got an affidavit, which was to guarantee that we needed to get a visa to come to America.
Well, this was in 1934.
In 1934, I made the application.
And by 1936, I was able to leave-- two years.
Things were getting worse, yes.
The Nuremberg Laws, exactly, yeah.
The Nuremberg Laws--
Well, in the business, it was very strange.
The business flourished because it was a very old family business.
The old customers of my-- which were mostly small department stores in, in smaller towns were still owned by the same people that had owned them before.
And 90% of them stayed with us.
And they also-- the only thing was, we had to send out everything in neutral package.

We could no longer have our name on the wrappings of the merchandise that was sent out, so in other words, so that the local Nazi leaders couldn't tell that this department store still bought from Jews.
The ridiculous thing is if they got merchandise with no name on it, it was just actually an indication that that was from a Jewish firm.

But the irony is that because there was large preparation for war-- Germany made at that time already, also they were hiding it-- led to the fact that people like us had merchandise and had merchandise to sell, and we're actually-- the business was actually prospering.
Woolen, woolen material, woolen mostly for men's clothing, woolen cloth.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So the business was really prospering, more or less, until Kristallnacht 1938 when it became obvious that it was impossible to carry on.
I was already in the United States.
Yes, well, the preparations, of course, took, took in much of-- took up much of my time.
And I had to go, first of all, to the American consul, which means days of waiting until one's number was called and then to be interviewed.
In those days, the, the, the-- there were not such masses of immigrants-- or emigrants-- yet, as were in later years because it was comparatively early, in 1934.
And in 19-- before I left in 1936, Germany [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tore up the Treaty of Versailles and started, against the treaty rules, to get-- to get-- to get militarized.
In fact, they marched into the demilitarized zone on the Rhineland.
And from then on, there was a, an order out from Hitler that nobody could leave.
No German man could leave Germany without getting a release from the military command in his area.
I was 28 years old.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
But they didn't take any Jews in the army.
But still, they-- in order-- when they wanted to leave the-- anybody who wanted to leave the country had to get this permission.
So this what happened actually only a few days before I was supposed to leave for Hamburg to take the boat to America.
And so that was in Munich.
I went to the headquarters of the German army in Munich.
And I was led into the room, into the office of a colonel, who was in charge.
And he invited me to sit down, and the adjutant who had brought me in had left the room.
And he said to me, this colonel said to me, why are you leaving Germany?
What is your excuse?
And I said, sir, you know I'm Jewish.
I don't need, really, an excuse.
I-- I'm, I'm supposed to leave.
And he said-- he called my name, Dispeker?
Dispeker?
Are you any relative to [?
Hans ?] Dispeker and to Sigfried Dispeker?
I said, yes, they're my father's first cousins.
And he said, they were damn good soldiers in the war.
Well, he said, if you have to leave, you have to leave.
I wish you luck.
And then he said to the adjutant-- he rang for the adjutant to come in, and he said to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] give this man the stamp.
So that was my-- the end of my career.
I'm only mentioning this because, you see, the attitude of this old German officer was certainly not hostile.
But they all knuckled under.
They had no other way.
They all had no other-- they had no way out.
Were you allowed to bring anything with you to America?
Yes, I was allowed to bring in some personal, first of all, personal effects, as many as I wanted to.
And I was one of the last people that could bring out an uninspected, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] uninspected lift, they called it.
It's a package of furniture.
Because later on, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody had to come and inspect everything that a Jew wanted to take out.
Like, for instance, when the pieces of the furniture of my great grandparents-- the Biedermeier furniture that we wanted to bring-- my parents brought with them in 1938.
But at that time, they had to pay full price.
What they took out, which was their own property, was assessed.
The value was assessed at very high prices.
And they had to pay that before they could take it out.
In other words, they had to rebuy everything that belonged to them already.
That was not the case yet.
I brought only personal, personal, personal effects.
No.
Then I came the United States.
Yes.
The boat was already crowded with refugees.
And it was an American boat because I wanted to be in America as soon as possible.
So I knew as soon I entered the American ship in Hamburg, I was in America, in a way.
Also, at that time, Ellis Island still existed.

And I didn't know, but everything went very smooth with the exception-- I mean, it was-- On the boat, we had-- first of all, we met many people who came from small cities, smaller towns in Germany who had gone through already at that time through beatings, murder.
And their experience has been much worse than that in the big-- in the big cities because nobody checked on it.
I mean, what they did to, to Jewish people in the-- in the country was much more unobserved and even wilder and more cruel than what they did in the cities.
I heard a lot of stories, yeah.
And then I landed--
--in New York.
And I was picked up by some friends who had already been there before me.
And well, those were the days in which it was actually possible to get a job.
And I, I, I got a job to nail-- to nail packing cases in the basement of a new-- of a Seventh Avenue fabric wholesaler.
And within a week, I was-- within a week, I was working.
School English.
I had been in England for a year as a young man.
And I spoke English with a German and English accent.
[LAUGHS]
I stayed in New York till 19-- from 1936 to 1940.
Oh, yes.
Oh, absolutely.
Not only-- I wrote them practically every day.
And we, we had to go to the-- we saw to it that our letters went on the outgoing boats.
There were still listings in those days.
There was no air mail.
So we-- there were listings in the when the mail closed for, let's say, the Washington or the Manhattan or the Bremen or the-- any of these ships that went.
And we were always sure that our letters went.
There were no telephone calls to Europe.
That was unheard of.
I got mail from my parents.
It was sometimes censored, not always.
But we always had to write as if it were censored because there was an enormous amount of fear that had still-- that was pervading the whole atmosphere.
That fear was so great that I even, when I once was free in America, I still remember that we used to talk always in hushed voices when anything slightly political came up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That took a long time to assimilate to a free society.
Well, at that time I was still hoping that they will soon come.
And I, I-- we were working on their emigration, and I was telling them that it wasn't so terrible to come to America, that they would probably be glad to be here.
And they were willing, and, and, and in fact, they worked on-- began to work on it.
And only after the Kristallnacht, it was made impossible because my father was then taken from his home.
And also he was a sick, old man, really.
He was not that old, but he wasn't well.
He was taken to Dachau with every Jewish man from Munich or from any German city-- not all to Dachau, but they were all taken to concentration camps.
And they were treated horribly there.
It was a cold November night.
They had to shed all their clothes and stand shivering for hours naked in the-- in the open air.
My uncle, my father's brother, who was with him.
He was in the same-- in the same group, arrested together with my father.
They only lived a few blocks away from each other, and he was in the same group.
And the doctors had no-- there was a couple of Jewish doctors in the concentration camp also together with my father.
When my father started to get sick, they couldn't take care of him because they had no way of, of pasteurizing anything, or, or any instruments or anything with them.

And so after the 11 days, when my father and my uncle and other men who had been in the First World War-- as soldiers in the front lines-- if you were not in the front lines, if you were in the-- in the reserve in the back, didn't count.
They were released from the camp.
And my uncle brought my father home, and he died nine, nine hours later.
My uncle survived.
Because they--
Yeah.
Because they had been soldiers in the front lines in World War I.
My father had the Iron Cross.
My uncle had the Iron Cross, which was a famous German medal and for, for bravery.
And that was the gratitude of the fatherland.
So they sent him home to die.
The news of Kristallnacht, which was the 9th of November-- we heard already in the on the 10th.
The direct news came with a wire from a family member, a telegram that said, Sigmund and Otto-- which were my father and his brother-- not at home.
News will follow.
And not at home meant that they were in a concentration camp.
We understood this language that had developed.
Well, no, not an established code, but a code developed that-- sometimes it even didn't work.
I-- my mother wrote me a letter that she-- my mother sent me some packages of German delicacies sometimes to New York, things that she thought you can't get in America.
And I accepted it always gladly because it was a piece of home.
And once she wrote, I sent you-- I know you like to drink cocoa.
I'm sending you some Swiss cocoa in the next package.
So I said to myself, I don't even like to drink cocoa.
What is she to warn me about the cocoa?
So I took the cocoa to my future mother-in-law, the girl that I dated.
And I said to her, can you use cocoa?
She said, yeah, we use it.
So I gave her the cocoa.
But she didn't use the cocoa.
And finally she said, nobody around his seems to want cocoa.
She had a cleaning woman from Holland whom she asked, do you like cocoa?
And she said, yeah, I do.
She said, I have a-- I have a can of powdered cocoa here.
You can take it home.
Well, the story was then my mother and father had put four pieces of gold-- 20 Mark gold pieces, that were worth a fortune-- on the bottom of that cocoa.
But she couldn't write it.
So that, that-- those gold pieces ended up with that family in Holland, entirely unprogrammed.
But this was the kind of thing that happened because people couldn't speak up what they really were doing.
Oh, it was devastating.
It was absolutely devastating.
And we-- we, we did-- I was even-- I was supposed to get married in, in early December and was looking forward to my parents coming to America.
And the fact that that had happened was absolutely-- you can't describe because we heard everything about the burning of the synagogues, the looting, the murdering, the, the arresting.
And to know that my father was there, and my uncle.
And all my relatives that were still in Germany were in concentration camps, the males, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was, was horrible.
Then we were waiting for news, and then I-- we got the telegram that my father had died.
And that was all.
